title: Masha and a Beautiful Tree
description: The girl named Masha was walking in the forest and found a complete binary tree of height $$$n$$$ and a permutation $$$p$$$ of length $$$m=2^n$$$. A complete binary tree of height $$$n$$$ is a rooted tree such that every vertex except the leaves has exactly two sons, and the length of the path from the root to any of the leaves is $$$n$$$. The picture below shows the complete binary tree for $$$n=2$$$. A permutation is an array consisting of $$$n$$$ different integers from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$. For example, [$$$2,3,1,5,4$$$] is a permutation, but [$$$1,2,2$$$] is not ($$$2$$$ occurs twice), and [$$$1,3,4$$$] is also not a permutation ($$$n=3$$$, but there is $$$4$$$ in the array). Let's enumerate $$$m$$$ leaves of this tree from left to right. The leaf with the number $$$i$$$ contains the value $$$p_i$$$ ($$$1 \le i \le m$$$). For example, if $$$n = 2$$$, $$$p = [3, 1, 4, 2]$$$, the tree will look like this: Masha considers a tree beautiful if the values in its leaves are ordered from left to right in increasing order. In one operation, Masha can choose any non-leaf vertex of the tree and swap its left and right sons (along with their subtrees). For example, if Masha applies this operation to the root of the tree discussed above, it will take the following form: Help Masha understand if she can make a tree beautiful in a certain number of operations. If she can, then output the minimum number of operations to make the tree beautiful .
input_spec: The first line contains single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — number of test cases. In each test case, the first line contains an integer $$$m$$$ ($$$1 \le m \le 262144$$$), which is a power of two  — the size of the permutation $$$p$$$. The second line contains $$$m$$$ integers: $$$p_1, p_2, \dots, p_m$$$ ($$$1 \le p_i \le m$$$) — the permutation $$$p$$$. It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$m$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$$3 \cdot 10^5$$$.
output_spec: For each test case in a separate line, print the minimum possible number of operations for which Masha will be able to make the tree beautiful or -1 , if this is not possible.

routes.name: Recursive divide and conquer on array halves
routes.description: Treat the array as an implicit complete binary tree. Recursively process left and right halves bottom-up, deciding at each internal node whether to swap its two children based on the min/max values of each subtree.

observations.title: The array maps directly to an implicit binary tree
observations.description: The permutation of length m = 2^n is the leaf layer of a complete binary tree. The left child of any subtree spanning [l, r) covers [l, mid) and the right child covers [mid, r) where mid = (l+r)/2. You never need to build an explicit tree -- just recurse on array ranges.

hints.nudge: How does a complete binary tree's structure relate to splitting an array in half repeatedly?
hints.question: If the leaves are p[0..m), what subarray corresponds to the left subtree of the root? The right subtree? How does this recurse?
hints.formal: Define solve(l, r) operating on p[l..r). Base case: r - l == 1 (leaf). Otherwise mid = (l+r)/2, left subtree is [l, mid), right subtree is [mid, r). This implicit representation avoids building a tree data structure.

observations.title: Process bottom-up: solve children before parent
observations.description: A swap at a node exchanges its entire left and right subtrees. To minimize total swaps, first recursively sort each child's subtree independently, then decide whether to swap at this node. The order matters: you must fix the children before you can evaluate the parent.

hints.nudge: If you swap at a parent node first, how does that affect what the children still need to do?
hints.question: Can the decision at a parent node ever be affected by not having sorted the children first? What order of processing gives a clean greedy choice?
hints.formal: Recursively call solve(l, mid) and solve(mid, r) first. After both return, the left and right halves are each internally sorted (if possible). Only then decide whether to swap the two halves at this level.

observations.title: Swap decision: compare max of left half vs min of right half
observations.description: After recursively sorting both halves, each half is internally sorted. To decide if a swap is needed, check whether max(left) < min(right) (already correct) or max(right) < min(left) (need to swap). If neither holds, it is impossible.

hints.nudge: After sorting both halves, what single comparison tells you whether the left half should come before the right half?
hints.question: If the left half contains values {5,6,7,8} and the right half contains {1,2,3,4}, what should you do? What if left is {1,3,5,7} and right is {2,4,6,8}?
hints.formal: Let maxL = max(p[l..mid]), minR = min(p[mid..r)). If maxL < minR: no swap needed. Else let maxR = max(p[mid..r)), minL = min(p[l..mid)). If maxR < minL: swap the two halves, add 1 to the count. Otherwise: return -1 (impossible).

observations.title: Impossibility arises when halves have interleaved values
observations.description: A swap only exchanges two entire blocks. If the values in the left and right subtrees are interleaved (e.g., left has {1,4} and right has {2,3}), no sequence of swaps at any level can sort them. The values within each subtree at every level must form a contiguous range after recursive sorting.

hints.nudge: A swap moves an entire subtree. Can a swap ever separate two values that are in the same subtree?
hints.question: Suppose the left half ends up with values {1,4} and the right half with {2,3}. Neither ordering (left-right or right-left) produces a sorted result. Why does this mean the answer is -1?
hints.formal: At each node, after recursively sorting children, the left and right halves are each sorted arrays. For the combined array to be sortable by a single swap-or-not decision, one half must contain all values less than every value in the other half. That is, the two halves must be a partition into two contiguous ranges of the permutation.

observations.title: Total complexity is O(m log m) via min/max tracking
observations.description: At each recursive level, you need the min and max of each half. Rather than scanning the subarray each time, return (min, max) from each recursive call. Each element is visited O(log m) times across all levels, giving O(m log m) total work.

hints.nudge: How can you avoid recomputing max and min from scratch at every level?
hints.question: If each recursive call returns the min and max of its subarray, how cheaply can the parent compute its own min and max after a potential swap?
hints.formal: solve(l, r) returns (min_val, max_val, swap_count) or -1. Base case: (p[l], p[l], 0). Recursive case: get (minL, maxL, cntL) and (minR, maxR, cntR) from children. If maxL < minR: return (minL, maxR, cntL+cntR). If maxR < minL: swap the subarrays, return (minR, maxL, cntL+cntR+1). Else: return -1. Total: O(m log m).
